question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed two explicit design-choice questions (ID scheme, argparse vs manual) and a follow-up about idempotency. Most major decision points engaged the user, though there were no questions during the actual code walkthrough.

question_quality.rationale: The ID scheme question hits the actual crux — the positional-ID footgun where rm shifts indices is exactly the substantive trade-off worth surfacing. The argparse choice is also a real fork. The Pair even pushed back when the user picked the worse option, surfacing the correctness consequence.

scaffolding.rationale: Logical progression: data model → arg parsing → implementation → review. The ID footgun was revealed via a concrete example rather than spoiled. Slight weakness: the second decision (argparse) is less consequential and the code dump at the end has no embedded checkpoints.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Conversational flow is clean, with explicit OPTIONS blocks and warm but efficient tone. The Pair appropriately challenged the user's first answer rather than rubber-stamping it. Slightly mechanical OPTIONS formatting but actionable throughout.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code works correctly across all four subcommands with persistent IDs, matching baseline functionality. Baseline additionally demonstrated extension via an edit command, but core requirements are equivalently satisfied.

friction.rationale: 4 turns vs baseline's 2, ~87s vs ~79s — only marginally longer, with a meaningful pedagogical payoff (the ID-shifting gotcha was genuinely instructive and changed the user's design). Proportional trade-off.
